strategies.hypothesis: A single independent answer is the minimum-cost baseline.
strategies.name: Direct One

strategies.hypothesis: A small independent plurality is a stable low-cost baseline.
strategies.name: Five-Vote Plurality

strategies.hypothesis: A five-answer bank paired with the validated three-reviewer falsification mechanism may retain much of the champion's accuracy at eight calls per case rather than twelve; earlier five-solver repair systems reached 0.417–0.500 pooled accuracy.
strategies.name: Efficient Five-Bank Falsifying Repair

strategies.hypothesis: This is a transparent replication of the earlier nine-solver, three-falsifier mechanism, restoring review on any disagreement instead of trusting a strict but potentially correlated majority. It tests whether the extra oracle coverage of nine bases can improve the weakest family when overconfident wrong pluralities are still reviewed. Results matching prior operational fields should be pooled under this mechanism, not treated as a renamed invention.
strategies.name: Nine Solvers plus Three Falsifying Repairers

strategies.hypothesis: The first panel of this exact mechanism retained four of the champion's five correct outcomes with one reviewer and no harmful reversals. A sealed replication tests whether its favorable correction-to-cost ratio is stable rather than a single-panel fluctuation.
strategies.name: One-Review Five-Bank Falsifying Repair

strategies.hypothesis: A sequential chain can turn reviewer disagreement into accumulated evidence: each reviewer sees the preceding answer and critique, attempts to falsify its decisive claims, and passes a revised exact proposal forward.
strategies.name: Sequential Cross-Examination
